titleStmt.title: The Minister in Finland ( (Schoenfeld) ) to the Secretary of State

div.head: The Minister in Finland ( Schoenfeld ) to the Secretary of State
div.p: 302. In conversation today with Minister of Commerce Tanner he told me that his return to Cabinet had been at urgent instance of President Ryti and that since in present circumstances his Ministry was not very busy he was able to devote himself to consultative work as principal representative of Social Democratic Party. This evidently signifies that President felt need for Tanner’s recognized abilities and experience as well as liberal point of view in councils of government.

Tanner told me he expected that with completion of military operations now proceeding and involving movement of Finnish troops
along both shores of Lake Ladoga to meet German forces advancing against Leningrad and with development of easterly movement of Finnish forces into northern Karelia it would probably be policy of Finnish Government to hold occupied territory pending clarification of general military and political situation. Tanner professed ignorance of German intentions following completion of their advance into Russia and said Finnish policy could not be clearly formulated until German intentions were known. He supposed, though he said he did not know, it would be German policy to break western Russia up into various political units. He did not indicate he expected formal peace conference to terminate present hostilities and seemed to think rather that settlement affecting Finland would have to await conclusion of general war until which time Finnish occupation of Russian territory would be maintained. He seemed to think Soviet regime would collapse following defeat of Red army.

Tanner confirmed Foreign Minister’s statement reported in my No. 292 of July 16 that Government did not approve Field Marshal Mannerheim’s recent order of day referring to liberation of eastern Karelia and told me that no decisions had been taken by Government on such territorial questions though all Finns were glad that this time hostilities were not being conducted entirely within Finnish territorial limits.

Tanner indicated clear understanding of Anglo-American policy regarding support of Russians against Germans though he said frankly that he considered danger of Bolshevism a greater threat than Nazism and repeated what I have reported previously that in Finnish view if choice has to be made between the two ideologies Finns would prefer Nazi brand. Tanner said he anticipated that further revolution would follow defeat of Germany and that it would be natural to expect it to take form of Bolshevism which would be grave calamity for Europe and especially for Finland.

Tanner gave me to understand that he, no less than President Ryti, is conscious of danger of German penetration here but asserted, as Witting on July 6, that thus far German Government had been very correct in its relations with Finland and only necessary military understandings have been entered into. Tanner on his record can not be considered favorable to Nazi political ideas and methods. I am convinced that his return to Government is intended as measure of precaution against those elements in Government itself and in the country who favor full acceptance of so-called new order and are dazzled by prospect of great territorial gains from Russia.
